blocks.content: They walked. The neighborhood was doing what it always did on the Fourth — kids with sparklers, a radio going somewhere, the smell of someone else's cookout still on the air. The light was low and golden.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I walked this street every day for six years.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I know. I was usually ahead of you.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You were always ahead of me. You walked too fast.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I walk at a normal pace.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You have long legs. That's not a normal pace for everyone.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: In sixth grade I told you and you slowed down for exactly one block and then forgot.
He slowed. She caught up. He reached for her hand without thinking about it and she let him take it without making it a thing.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: This whole summer feels like something I knew was coming but still didn't know how to prepare for.
They turned the corner at the end of his block. The field opened up ahead of them — wide and dark at the edges and lit up in the middle with lanterns Eli had clearly spent real time on.
The cooler was, in fact, unreasonably large.
